findings.message: PII logged in plaintext: the caller's email address is passed directly to logging.info() with no pseudonymisation, hashing, or masking. Email addresses are personal data under GDPR Art. 4(1); logging them at INFO level sends them to every log sink (stdout, log aggregators, SIEM) with no retention limit or deletion path, violating the storage-limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e)) and making right-to-erasure (Art. 17) compliance impractical.
findings.suggestion: Never log raw email addresses. Use a one-way hash (e.g. hashlib.sha256(email.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]) or a stable pseudonym for correlation. Move sensitive-operation records to a dedicated audit log with a defined retention and erasure policy rather than the application INFO stream.

findings.message: Email address accepted as a plain query parameter: query parameters appear in access logs, reverse-proxy logs, browser history, and Referer headers, creating additional uncontrolled PII sinks beyond the explicit logging call. Passing PII in a URL query string violates data-minimisation and confidentiality expectations (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) and (f)).
findings.suggestion: Accept user identity via a request header (e.g. X-User-ID carrying an opaque internal identifier) or a JWT claim rather than a raw email query parameter. Avoid placing PII in URLs.

findings.message: Sensitive search operation has no audit log: the endpoint performs a personalised search tied to a user identity but records it only to the application INFO stream, not to a tamper-evident audit log. GDPR Art. 30 (records of processing) and Art. 5(2) (accountability) require that processing of personal data be demonstrably documented; an INFO log with no retention policy, integrity controls, or deletion-audit path does not satisfy this.
findings.suggestion: Emit a structured audit event to a dedicated audit sink (separate logger or audit table) capturing a pseudonymous user identifier, the operation, and a timestamp. Define and enforce a retention window with an erasure procedure that can be triggered on a right-to-erasure request.
